q: Consider an ideal fluid there must be laminar flow and no viscosity.
For an ideal fluid, the density of the fluid is variable throughout the pipe.
The equation of continuity for fluid flow states $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$.
The mass flowing in section 1 must be half the mass flowing in section 2 in a constant volume flow rate system.
The larger the area of the pipe, the slower is the speed.
The speed of water spraying from a hose increases by making the hose area larger.
Volume flow rate $\frac{V}{t}$ is the same throughout the pipe in an ideal fluid flow.
The equation $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$ is known as Bernoulli's equation for fluid flow.
The equation of continuity states that the liquid entering one end of the pipe leaves from the other end with a constant volume flow rate.
In the equation of continuity, $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ represent the density of liquid at section 1 and section 2 respectively.
The smaller the area of the pipe, the faster is the speed of the fluid.
Equation of continuity is valid for turbulent flow with high internal friction.
All the liquid that flow through section 1 (larger cross sectional area $A_{1}$) flow through section 2 (smaller cross sectional area $A_{2}$).
According to the equation of continuity, area and speed are directly proportional to each other.
Constant volume flow rate is maintained in a system where $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$.
The density of liquid is constant for any pressure provided the volume is kept constant.
